judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports independently hit all five expectations with essentially equivalent depth and correct citations; Report 2 additionally surfaces a bonus finding (unsigned token verification and missing CI) not required by the rubric, giving it a slight edge in thoroughness without any difference in expectation coverage.

report_1.evidence: Postgres RLS policies are, by documented default behavior, not applied to the table owner — only to other roles — unless the table is explicitly altered with FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY. ... Currently, the API's runtime DATABASE_URL connects as juniper_owner — the schema-owning role — in docker-compose.yml:5, .env.example:1, and infra/main.tf:2

report_2.evidence: the owner of a table is exempt from that table's row-level security policies by default, independent of whether the role has the BYPASSRLS attribute or is a superuser — that owner-exemption is a separate mechanism, disabled only by explicitly running ALTER TABLE ... FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY. ... juniper/docker-compose.yml:5, juniper/.env.example:1, and juniper/infra/main.tf:2 all currently set DATABASE_URL=postgresql://juniper_owner:ownerpw@db:5432/juniper

report_1.evidence: Commit b2a23d2a527cd13589163a40ef51b2eb2a430af6 — "ROTA-214 run migrations from the api container on boot"... .env.example: juniper_app:apppw → juniper_owner:ownerpw / docker-compose.yml: juniper_app:apppw → juniper_owner:ownerpw / infra/main.tf: juniper_app:apppw → juniper_owner:ownerpw

report_2.evidence: in commit b2a23d2a527cd13589163a40ef51b2eb2a430af6 — "ROTA-214 run migrations from the api container on boot." ... b2a23d2 changed .env.example, docker-compose.yml, and infra/main.tf to postgresql://juniper_owner:ownerpw@db:5432/juniper

report_1.evidence: Restore role separation for the runtime connection — revert docker-compose.yml:5, .env.example:1, and infra/main.tf:2 so the API's request-serving DATABASE_URL uses juniper_app (non-owner...), not juniper_owner. ... Force RLS at the table, as defense-in-depth — add ALTER TABLE appointments FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;

report_2.evidence: Primary fix — ...revert the runtime DATABASE_URL to the least-privilege role, juniper_app (or a dedicated non-owner runtime role), for anything serving live traffic. ... Defense in depth — ...add ALTER TABLE appointments FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;

report_1.evidence: Add an explicit application-layer tenant filter — in backend/app/routes/appointments.py:16-19, add WHERE tenant_id = :tid using the tenant from claims, instead of relying solely on the DB session GUC + RLS.

report_2.evidence: add an explicit WHERE tenant_id = :tid to the query, using the same tenant id already resolved in deps.py. ... isolation should never depend on exactly one mechanism (RLS alone) working correctly.

report_1.evidence: Add a startup/health self-check in the API: query pg_roles.rolbypassrls and compare current_user against pg_tables.tableowner for appointments; refuse to start (or page) if the runtime role is the table owner or has BYPASSRLS. This converts a silent data leak into a loud boot-time failure.

report_2.evidence: Boot-time assertion: on API startup, run a SELECT rolbypassrls... style check ... and refuse to start if the connected role is the table owner or has BYPASSRLS.
